sources.full_name: CPMI-IOSCO Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures
sources.coverage_note: All 24 PFMI principles and 5 responsibilities for authorities.

provisions.title: Principle 1 - Legal Basis
provisions.text: An FMI should have a well-founded, clear, transparent, and enforceable legal basis for each material aspect of its activities in all relevant jurisdictions. The legal basis should provide a high degree of certainty for each material aspect of an FMI's activities, including netting, settlement finality, collateral arrangements, default procedures, and the rights and obligations of the FMI and its participants.

provisions.title: Principle 2 - Governance
provisions.text: An FMI should have governance arrangements that are clear and transparent, promote the safety and efficiency of the FMI, and support the stability of the broader financial system, other relevant public interest considerations, and the objectives of relevant stakeholders. The governance arrangements should include clear and direct lines of responsibility and accountability; documented decision-making processes; and mechanisms to address conflicts of interest.

provisions.title: Principle 3 - Framework for Comprehensive Risk Management
provisions.text: An FMI should have a sound risk-management framework for comprehensively managing legal, credit, liquidity, operational, and other risks. This framework should include appropriate governance arrangements, policies, procedures, and systems to identify, measure, monitor, and manage the range of risks that arise in or are borne by the FMI. Risk management policies should be regularly reviewed and updated.

provisions.title: Principle 4 - Credit Risk
provisions.text: An FMI should effectively measure, monitor, and manage its credit exposures to participants and those arising from its payment, clearing, and settlement processes. An FMI should maintain sufficient financial resources to cover its credit exposure to each participant fully with a high degree of confidence. A CCP that is involved in activities with a more-complex risk profile or that is systemically important in multiple jurisdictions should maintain additional financial resources sufficient to cover a wide range of potential stress scenarios.

provisions.title: Principle 5 - Collateral
provisions.text: An FMI that requires collateral to manage its or its participants' credit exposure should accept collateral with low credit, liquidity, and market risks. An FMI should also set and enforce appropriately conservative haircuts and concentration limits. The FMI should value collateral on a regular basis, at least daily, and subject collateral to adequate stress testing.

provisions.title: Principle 6 - Margin
provisions.text: A CCP should cover its credit exposures to its participants for all products through an effective margin system that is risk-based and regularly reviewed. Margin should be set to cover potential future exposure to participants in the interval between the last margin collection and the close out of positions following a participant default. Initial margin models should be validated at least monthly.

provisions.title: Principle 7 - Liquidity Risk
provisions.text: An FMI should effectively measure, monitor, and manage its liquidity risk. An FMI should maintain sufficient liquid resources in all relevant currencies to effect same-day and, where appropriate, intraday and multiday settlement of payment obligations with a high degree of confidence under a wide range of potential stress scenarios. A CCP should maintain liquid resources sufficient to cover the liquidity shortfall from the default of at least one (or for systemically important CCPs, two) participant(s) that would generate the largest aggregate liquidity obligation.

provisions.title: Principle 8 - Settlement Finality
provisions.text: An FMI should provide clear and certain final settlement, at a minimum by the end of the value date. Where necessary or preferable, an FMI should provide final settlement intraday or in real time. An FMI should clearly define the point at which settlement is final and should complete final settlement no later than the end of the value date.

provisions.title: Principle 9 - Money Settlements
provisions.text: An FMI should conduct its money settlements in central bank money where practical and available. If central bank money is not used, an FMI should minimise and strictly control the credit and liquidity risk arising from the use of commercial bank money. The FMI should monitor, manage, and limit its credit and liquidity risks arising from commercial settlement banks.

provisions.title: Principle 10 - Physical Deliveries
provisions.text: An FMI should clearly state its obligations with respect to the delivery of physical instruments or commodities and should identify, monitor, and manage the risks associated with such physical deliveries. The FMI should have rules and procedures that clearly state the FMI's obligations and the obligations of the participants with respect to physical delivery.

provisions.title: Principle 11 - Central Securities Depositories
provisions.text: A CSD should have appropriate rules and procedures to help ensure the integrity of securities issues and minimise and manage the risks associated with the safekeeping and transfer of securities. A CSD should maintain securities in an immobilised or dematerialised form for their transfer by book entry. The CSD should protect the assets of its participants and those of their customers by prohibiting overdrafts or debit balances in securities accounts.

provisions.title: Principle 12 - Exchange-of-Value Settlement Systems
provisions.text: If an FMI settles transactions that involve the settlement of two linked obligations (for example, securities or foreign exchange transactions), it should eliminate principal risk by conditioning the final settlement of one obligation upon the final settlement of the other (delivery versus payment, or payment versus payment).

provisions.title: Principle 13 - Participant-Default Rules and Procedures
provisions.text: An FMI should have effective and clearly defined rules and procedures to manage a participant default. These rules and procedures should be designed to ensure that the FMI can take timely action to contain losses and liquidity pressures and continue to meet its obligations. Key elements include: definition of a default event; actions the FMI may take upon a default; procedures for the management and close-out of the defaulting participant's obligations; and the order in which financial resources may be used.

provisions.title: Principle 14 - Segregation and Portability
provisions.text: A CCP should have rules and procedures that enable the segregation and portability of positions of a participant's customers and the collateral provided to the CCP with respect to those positions. Segregation should protect customer positions and collateral from the default or insolvency of the participant through which the customer accesses the CCP. Portability should enable the transfer of customer positions and collateral from a defaulting participant to a surviving participant.

provisions.title: Principle 15 - General Business Risk
provisions.text: An FMI should identify, monitor, and manage its general business risk and hold sufficient liquid net assets funded by equity to cover potential general business losses so that it can continue operations and services as a going concern if those losses materialise. Liquid net assets should at all times be sufficient to ensure a recovery or orderly wind-down of critical operations and services. An FMI should hold liquid net assets funded by equity equal to at least six months of current operating expenses.

provisions.title: Principle 16 - Custody and Investment Risks
provisions.text: An FMI should safeguard its own and its participants' assets and minimise the risk of loss on and delay in access to these assets. An FMI's investments should be in instruments with minimal credit, market, and liquidity risks. Investment policies should be approved by the board and should be consistent with the FMI's risk management strategy.

provisions.title: Principle 17 - Operational Risk
provisions.text: An FMI should identify the plausible sources of operational risk, both internal and external, and mitigate their impact through the use of appropriate systems, policies, procedures, and controls. Systems should be designed to ensure a high degree of security and operational reliability and should have adequate, scalable capacity. The FMI should have a comprehensive business continuity plan that addresses events posing a significant risk of disrupting operations, including recovery within two hours and completion of settlement by the end of the day of disruption.

provisions.title: Principle 18 - Access and Participation Requirements
provisions.text: An FMI should have objective, risk-based, and publicly disclosed criteria for participation, which permit fair and open access. Requirements should be justified in terms of the safety and efficiency of the FMI and the markets it serves, tailored to the FMI's specific risks, and reviewed regularly. An FMI should monitor compliance with participation requirements on an ongoing basis and have procedures for managing non-compliance.

provisions.title: Principle 19 - Tiered Participation Arrangements
provisions.text: An FMI should identify, monitor, and manage the material risks to the FMI arising from tiered participation arrangements. Tiered arrangements exist when some parties rely on other participants (direct participants) to access the FMI. The FMI should gather information to identify, monitor, and manage risks arising from multi-level participation, including the proportion of activity conducted by indirect participants and the dependencies of direct participants on indirect participant activity.

provisions.title: Principle 20 - FMI Links
provisions.text: An FMI that establishes a link with one or more FMIs should identify, monitor, and manage link-related risks. CSD links should include adequate delivery versus payment arrangements. CCPs that establish links should identify, monitor, and manage the potential credit, liquidity, and operational risks arising from the link. Links should be underpinned by a sound legal framework.

provisions.title: Principle 21 - Efficiency and Effectiveness
provisions.text: An FMI should be efficient and effective in meeting the requirements of its participants and the markets it serves. The FMI should have clearly defined goals and objectives that are measurable and achievable. The FMI should have mechanisms for regular review of its efficiency and effectiveness.

provisions.title: Principle 22 - Communication Procedures and Standards
provisions.text: An FMI should use, or at a minimum accommodate, relevant internationally accepted communication procedures and standards in order to facilitate efficient payment, clearing, settlement, and recording. The use of internationally accepted communication standards (such as ISO 20022) and procedures helps to ensure interoperability between FMIs and their participants.

provisions.title: Principle 23 - Disclosure of Rules, Procedures, and Market Data
provisions.text: An FMI should have clear and comprehensive rules and procedures and should provide sufficient information to enable participants to have an accurate understanding of the risks, fees, and other material costs they incur by participating in the FMI. All relevant rules and key procedures should be publicly disclosed. An FMI should complete and publicly disclose responses to the CPMI-IOSCO Disclosure Framework on a regular basis.

provisions.title: Principle 24 - Disclosure of Market Data by Trade Repositories
provisions.text: A trade repository should provide timely and accurate data to relevant authorities and the public in line with their respective needs. Data provided to authorities should be comprehensive, covering positions, transactions, and other relevant information. Data provided to the public should support market transparency and should not compromise the confidentiality of individual participant data.

provisions.title: Responsibility A - Regulation, Supervision and Oversight
provisions.text: FMIs should be subject to appropriate and effective regulation, supervision, and oversight by a central bank, market regulator, or other relevant authority. Authorities should clearly define and publicly disclose the criteria used to identify which FMIs should be subject to regulation, supervision, and oversight.

provisions.title: Responsibility B - Regulatory Powers and Resources
provisions.text: Central banks, market regulators, and other relevant authorities should have the powers and resources to carry out effectively their responsibilities in regulating, supervising, and overseeing FMIs. This includes the power to: obtain information; assess observance of applicable principles and standards; require changes to an FMI's rules, procedures, or operations; and impose corrective measures or sanctions.

provisions.title: Responsibility C - Disclosure of Policies
provisions.text: Central banks, market regulators, and other relevant authorities should clearly define and disclose their regulatory, supervisory, and oversight policies with respect to FMIs. Authorities should publicly disclose the criteria used in identifying which FMIs are subject to regulation, and the standards applied.

provisions.title: Responsibility D - Application of PFMI
provisions.text: Central banks, market regulators, and other relevant authorities should adopt the CPMI-IOSCO Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures and apply them consistently. Authorities should regularly review the consistency of their frameworks with the PFMI and should publish their assessment methodology.

provisions.title: Responsibility E - Cooperation Among Authorities
provisions.text: Central banks, market regulators, and other relevant authorities should cooperate with each other, both domestically and internationally, as appropriate, in promoting the safety and efficiency of FMIs. Cooperative oversight arrangements should be established for FMIs that are material to multiple jurisdictions.

definitions.definition: A multilateral system among participating institutions, including the operator of the system, used for the purposes of clearing, settling, or recording payments, securities, derivatives, or other financial transactions. Includes payment systems, CSDs, SSSs, CCPs, and trade repositories.

An entity that interposes itself between counterparties to contracts traded in one or more financial markets, becoming the buyer to every seller and the seller to every buyer, thereby ensuring the performance of open contracts.
A securities settlement mechanism that links a securities transfer and a funds transfer in such a way as to ensure that delivery occurs if and only if the corresponding payment occurs.
